nativeRange.habitat: Shallow lagoon and offshore coral reefs at depths of 0–10 m

decorPreferences: caves and crevices for hiding and territory establishment

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on meaty items (brine shrimp, mysis), herbivore preparations, algae, and flaked foods. In captivity, generally accepts high-quality marine flakes, pellets, and frozen foods with regular feedings once or twice daily.

avoidWith: docile species that cannot defend themselves

imageCandidates.notes: Debra Baker, 1801x1465. Sharp, well-lit lateral shot of a full-colour adult: electric-blue body with yellow-orange ventral/anal fins and blue face lines — all diagnostic taupou features clearly shown.
Debra Baker, 1566x1301. Sharp in-situ shot beside Acropora; vivid electric-blue body with orange dorsal edge, whole fish in frame. Different individual/setting for variety.
Debra Baker, 1734x1246. Sharp lateral view of a teal-blue juvenile with orange dorsal edge; correct ID, whole animal in frame.

summary: A vivid blue and yellow damselfish from the Western Pacific, generally considered easy to keep but tends to be aggressive — best housed with larger, semi-aggressive tankmates and kept singly.
careNotes:
The Fiji Blue Devil Damselfish (Chrysiptera taupou) is found in shallow lagoon and reef habitats across the Western Pacific, from the Coral Sea and northern Great Barrier Reef east to Samoa, including Fiji, Tonga, and parts of Indonesia.

A minimum tank of around 138 L is generally recommended, and more space tends to reduce aggression. Plenty of live rock and coral structures for territory establishment and hiding are broadly advised.

This species has been documented as reef-safe in most cases, with no reported instances of coral nipping. It is diurnal and inhabits the mid-water column near coral structures. Males have been observed guarding and aerating benthic egg clutches. Captive-bred specimens are available from some specialty suppliers. No venomous spines are present, and the species is not generally considered an escape risk.
breedingNotes: Oviparous; substrate spawning has been observed in captivity with distinct pairing. Males guard and aerate adhesive eggs deposited on hard substrate. Breeding in home aquaria has been documented, though rearing larvae through the planktonic stage tends to be challenging. Captive-bred specimens are occasionally available from specialty marine fish suppliers.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (8.2 cm FL), temperature range (25.2–29.4°C), native range (Western Pacific, Coral Sea to Samoa), taxonomy, and reproductive behavior. Note: the designated secondary URL (Reef2Reef thread) returned HTTP 403; FishBase was used as the first fallback from source-urls.json.

additional.name: Reeflings
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (24–27°C), salinity (SG 1.023–1.026), tank minimum (100 L), temperament (semi-aggressive/territorial), and reef safety. Temperature disagreement with LiveAquaria resolved by averaging across all sources: min 23.8°C, max 27.3°C.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperament (highly aggressive), salinity (~1.026), and tank minimum (200 L). Tank volume spread across sources (100–200 L) averaged to ~138 L. Temperament: 2 of 3 sources (LiveAquaria, ReefApp) rated aggressive vs. 1 semi-aggressive (Reeflings); 'aggressive' used.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous substrate spawner; males guard and aerate adhesive eggs. Breeding has been documented in captivity; larval rearing tends to be challenging. Captive-bred stock is occasionally available.
